speaker: interviewer
text: This is roll six with Silvia Martin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He's trying to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a lot of dare. The guys got a lot of, you know, instruments necessary for-- a saw and hammer and so on. And they were working on sawing the things out-- you know, the piece of wood. So that they could get out, and then stand on the couplers between the two trains, and then let-- sit down on the couplers, and let ourselves down under the train and let the train go over us. It was nerve-racking to hear them saw away, because it was not possible for anybody else to hear. But we didn't exactly know where were the soldiers with the guns, and where were-- how much noise did it make, compared with the train, the rolling of the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you had the other people who were watching us. Most of us wishing us luck, of course. But we found out we were only ones who had decided to jump. And I was wondering, did anybody else wanted to jump? I mean, there were old people, too. But we thought everybody would like to jump.

speaker: interviewee
text: The heat in there, and the smell-- because we had nothing to take the buckets out or anything-- I mean, it went on and on and on. It was absolutely incredible, really incredible. And I remember, they got that thing out, about this size. And the first one got out on the couplers, just to see, you know. And he was a Hungarian football player-- he was a big guy. And he came back and said, wonderful, no problem, there's so much room under the train. I mean, somebody twice his size-- certainly I could go under there. And then each of us tried to go out. And I remember when I was out there, I forgot why I was out there. I totally forgot-- fresh air. There was air. And I was just holding myself on, and then sat down on the couplers just to try. It wasn't the point to jump, yet, it was just to try. And got in again. And then I remember, I got very tired. And then there was-- I must have fallen asleep, because what woke me was the noise of guns. Rat-tat-tat-tat-ta, rat-tat-tat-tat-ta, rat-tat-tat-tat-ta. And as I got up and looked out that little hole, there were people jumping from other trains. And boom, boom, boom, boom, boom.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was like sewing, you know? Machine gun, all over the place. Falling, running, falling, running. The next thing was, the doors-- this thing-- flew open. And one of those German Nazis screaming, screaming, who jumped here? Who wanted to jump?

speaker: interviewee
text: End of the story was that they took from every train two people, whether they were among the ones who wanted to jump or not, and shot them, right. Young, old, didn't matter. And when there was a couple, you know, young couple, holding each other-- right through the heart. In our wagon, there was an old couple. And that Nazi said, you-- pointing at these two old people. She was half undressed and frightened. And she-- they wanted-- he wanted them out. And the one who was in charge of our group, he put himself in front of them. And he had a scene with that Nazi which was unbelievable-- Heinz was his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, 'You want to shoot somebody? Shoot me. Nobody wanted to jump here. Nobody. We-- we are dying from the stink and the smell, but nobody wanted to jump. There's nobody in there.' He fought with them, and he said, 'You want to kill somebody? Kill me. I'll see you in hell.' And he jumped the German.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's when the German looked inside-- meanwhile, the bucket flew over, the whole stink came out, everything came out. And this guy was so disgusted from what he saw that he walked away. And then he gave Heinz the permission to empty the bucket and clean up and so on. The woman fell over, the bucket fell on her, he-- she fell into somebody else's arm. It was a catastrophe. Meanwhile, this was open on the side. And while the scene was going on with Heinz and that Nazi, I stood against this-- holding, holding, that nobody could see that there was a thing missing. The board was out! [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I stood against it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And when this whole scene was over-- which lasted forever-- and they had honestly shot from every wagon two people, plus the ones who tried to escape. Did escape, I don't know how many. What happened is that, I'd fallen asleep. When we got to the spot where we wanted to jump-- where the train should not have stopped. The one who-- Erdmann went with us-- stopped the train. They knew the jumpers. They knew people would jump. They didn't keep on going, no matter how slowly. The train was stopped and stood there all night. So we were at the right spot, wrong time. The train never moved an inch. Even so, the people jumped. And of course, this is how it happened. That wasn't all. When this was finally closed, and we saw we would go on, my friends immediately tried to board this shut. And that is when a guy from outside said, hey, general, or whatever, look. This is open. The whole thing is open here. They tried to crawl out from here. And, you know, Jews always lie, and they should be shot just for lying, and so on. Let me show you, let me show you the boards. They didn't-- it won't-- it was like a coffin. Where they couldn't cut-- shut it enough. So half of it was gone in the wind. And when I moved away from it, this was going. And one guy from those easy beavers saw that, and wanted the guy to come and look that there was-- we had cut out a board. He didn't want to have anything to do with it anymore. And they went on. But what happened is, just as I sat down, there was a knife coming through. This guy, who said, the board is open-- he had put his knife through to see that it was open. So where I had stood just few seconds ago, there was this knife coming through. I was sitting on the floor and I thought then, well, it means that there's still a chance. Even though I wouldn't have known what. I didn't know there was a knife until much later. As the train started to go again, the other people in the car said, of course, you got to throw all these things out-- the hammer and saw. It's no good anymore. It will only cause us problems, because they're going to stop the train again and again and again. Didn't want to do it, they didn't want to do it yet. We thought, perhaps somewhere else. They stopped the train a second time to empty everything out, and we went under the train. We were told to go under the train, to do our business there. And there I could see how much room there was. There was so much room. I mean, an army could have been lying underneath. And at this moment, I still thought we could escape. I'd no idea where we were-- nothing. No idea where we were going, for that matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. I didn't know until I was-- I don't know how long already there. Nobody told us. I didn't even know what country we were driving through. Nothing. There was nothing to see. There was nothing to see. I know that everybody who was at this tiny little window was told to look out-- look out for whether you see a sign, a language, something which will tell you if it's Germany, if it's Russia, whatever. We didn't know. The second time, and it went on-- that trip went on-- people-- that couple which was almost killed, they came to Heinz, and of course, kissed his hand and thanked him and said not to do this again. It isn't-- they don't want him to die for them. And, you know, this is no way to live-- if God wants it now, we'll go. I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say, it's very strange. I know every detail. I don't know how many-- because you know, I fell asleep, I woke up, I fell asleep. The train stopped for such a long time, went on, stopped, went on. It seemed for-- for days and nights, for days and nights. We had no food, of course. Because we had to eat it all, otherwise it would have spoiled. Water ration was gone in no time at all. And there were so many wounded. Our wagon, our car, was the only one there was no-- I mean, sick, but no wounded. When it finally stopped, first of all, it was that you wanted to be close to the one you knew. Very close. And that, for the first time, I know that that woman on the bucket kept staring at me, you know, while we were trying to get out. I wore a tiny, tiny little slip and a bra and panties underneath. And she in this totally black, black thing-- you know, I don't know who looked more out of place or more ridiculous. It was such a weird-- totally like-- like-- from an asylum. You know, where they say, go on a party, go on a masquerade, and do the most craziest thing-- dress yourself in anything you want. And you can be as filthy and dirty as you want.

speaker: interviewer
text: And hunger and stink and smell-- and all the time this closed in. Can you imagine? This much room to look out? And you have to say, three minutes for you, and the next one goes. Three minutes for you to breathe some air.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we were told, please look. You must recognize something, so we know where we're going. And then one of them said at the window, I think I read a German sign. I think this is Germany. We're going-- we're driving through Germany. And then there was nothing anymore, for a long time. For a long, long time. It was really early in the morning-- it was nothing anymore. Everybody looked, we couldn't see. And then, very, very far away, we saw some figures. We thought they were men, in some, what looked like prison uniforms, carrying very heavy loads. You know, going like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what we were saying is, the most important thing is no longer where we are going-- where we are, but who is going to be there? SS? Or military? Because this is the life and death situation. Is it military? We have a chance. It may be a labor camp. If it is SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the cars stopped, and everybody was told to get out. And there again, were only rail tracks-- nothing. No tree. It was-- it wasn't even of this Earth. It wasn't even of this Earth. Totally desolate nothing. And then we saw them coming closer-- SS. All SS, SS, SS. Still didn't know where I was. None of us did. Heinz and Werner, my two friends, were taken away from me to help with the wounded. And then everybody was told to line up. Always in five-- I never understood why. Five, five, five. It's always row of five. And there were tracks on one side, and then there were people standing on one side. And we were told, who is tired should go on the tracks, they will be driven. And who is not so tired can walk. And the only reason that I went to the left is because Reina was standing there. And my two friends had already taken-- had been taken from me. So I wanted to be with someone I knew. And I went to the left. And then this beautiful man came. He looked like a matinee idol-- right stepped out of a movie, matinee. Not a bead of sweat on his face. I mean, also, they all always looked like they were just poured into their uniform. You know, like a window display. And he was very nice. And he also said, to the left and to the right. But yet, he did with, the blink of an eye, whom he didn't like-- even though they walked, they were standing where the ones who were supposed to walk. He said, you know, like this. To the truck. And there was a friend I had then-- opera singer. Wonderful opera singer-- had little kitten. And a mother she adored, and the mother adored her. And this mother and the kitten went on the train-- went on the truck, because the mother was tired. They were not in our wagon. And Fritzi was with me. And then we started marching. And the-- an SS guard, a female guard, and two male guards who walked with us, looked as bad as we do. One was full of-- I don't know, like infectious blood. Like thousands of mosquito bites in her head. And the uniform was lousy, and dragging their feet. I remember that, in the car, when I was in the wagon, in the cattle car-- when I was stepping outside, I stepped back once because I had my coat in there. I went back to get my coat-- my jewelry-filled coat, which I had brought all the way from Amsterdam to Belgium to Berlin, everywhere, in the cattle car, all the way to Auschwitz. Didn't know it was Auschwitz. And then we marched and we marched and we marched, and then I saw this big sign. Arbeit Macht Frei-- work makes free. And the interesting thing, the first thing I saw was, there was Entrance. I never saw a sign which said Exit. In all the time I was there, I was looking for exit-- there was no exit. There was only entrance.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you find there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't find out where Auschwitz was, or that we were in Auschwitz, until the next day. Until the very next day. Not that it meant anything to me at that time. I mean, I just know it was the end of the world. It was in a country which was no country. There were no trees, there was no sky, there was no heaven. There was nothing. Only a sun, which burnt-- which burned you. Cold at night, freezing cold at night, because it's kind of a desert climate. And something which does not belong. Not that it doesn't belong, but it doesn't belong to this Earth. It's somewhere else. It is absolutely-- I don't know where they found that place. Yes, I had heard, much, much later, that the place where they built Auschwitz on-- or Birkenau-- was originally, way back when, a battleground where Russian and, I don't know, somebody else, fought another war. And that these-- these barracks-- or stables, which they called our quarters-- had been stables for the horses. That on this earth, where we were walking on, underneath were a lot of buried Russian soldiers. That this was a battleground once.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened to you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were first-- I think they do this on purpose, really, to introduce you very quickly to-- that this is-- that hell is heaven compared to that.

speaker: interviewer
text: First, again, those typical [NON-ENGLISH] bureaus, where little girls sit with pretty dresses and clean faces and hair and so on, and ask name and address, and all this kind of thing, from one to the other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then you come into a big sauna-- it looks like a sports arena, you know, with benches going all the way up. And there was a woman which must have been 300 or 400 pounds, a former puff mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know what a puff is? Puff is the German word for, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, if you have, how shall I say that elegantly? It's not elegant. A whorehouse-- you know, a madam. A madam, this is what is, a madam.

speaker: interviewee
text: She had a big, big, in Hamburg-- she really ran a big-- and her, she was a prisoner, non-Jewish. And she had a big whip. And with this, she just whipped you in and out of this place. You got-- you need to get totally undressed. All inside of young, little, stupid, pimply SS guards who were laughing their heads off, because this was very funny. And they stepped on it, and they picked it-- they played football with my handbag. And, oh, the mirror broke, and I was thinking, seven years bad luck. And I know two pictures came out-- from Margit and Toni, and from Herbert, my fiance. And he put his heels in there, and he kicked it, like football playing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had no clothes, then we were put in the sauna. And while she snored her afternoon siesta, we were allowed to take a shower. By the time I went under, it was already gone. The water was off. A little bit of soap between us, and then we were dressed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in about five minutes or less, 120 women were dressed. I got a wool dress, which was so hot I can't even tell you. No shoes. And as an undergarment, I had a white, long-- it looked like a tablecloth. But it was, you know, what my great-grandmother used to wear-- old slip, coming down to here. Reina had an apron, that's all. And Fritzi got a regular sack. You know, a sack-- just this was missing. And then we were-- no. Then we lost our hair. Then we got into another room. Every other room had a purpose. And there were two girls-- one with a razor, the other one with a scissor, cutting your hair, completely. Just like a carpet, all colors-- higher and higher and higher. And somebody had said that I was an actress, which was a stupid place to say that, I must say. And she asked me whether I-- no, she asked me first whether I was a nurse. Because if I were a nurse, I could keep my hair that long. I said, I wasn't a nurse-- this is not something you can fake. And then she asked me whether I could play an instrument, because if I could play an instrument I could come into the orchestra and play. And that's another thing you can't fake. And I said, no. And then she said I was very stupid, and took every ounce of my hair off. And that is something very interesting. Something happens to women. I don't know about men-- but to women, something happens if you take their hair away. Suddenly you see them, marching ahead of you, where they walked very tall and straight, like this. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then to get kicked. And the ones who still manage to walk straight don't get kicked. Psychologically, you know right there and then, you've gotta look at them at all times. You're naked, walk by them naked. Walk straight, walk tall, look at them. The moment you're like this, you're in for it. All the time. These little guys stood by the door for no other purpose but to kick every woman who wasn't fast enough to get through the door into the next room.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after you lose your hair, they take everything. And suddenly you have nothing to hold on to anymore. Nothing. I had my comb, and somebody grabbed my comb. So there's nothing anymore. You don't know where to go with your arms, and-- and you see yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you got to-- you've got to look straight. And the opera singer did. She was very, very tall. And the best part were her legs, which were covered from the old sack. But her head looked like a melon. She looked grotesque. Totally grotesque. And still, she looked very tall. She walked very tall. And she didn't get kicked. And then they let us stand in the hot, boiling sun for hours on the-- what you call the camp street, the main street, what turned out to be Birkenau. And that is for the purpose to know that whatever you thought you've seen already before, or gone through with it, forget it. It's nothing. Its nothing. It's not even the beginning. There you see colonies, hundreds and hundreds, walking down the camp street. Not only naked, but the muselmann, they got to call-- the living death. They cling to each other, they drag each other, they hold on to each other. They all walk slowly to the showers. Before they are going to die, they have to have a bath. Because that is very important, that they are clean. Now, these were people, I have no idea whether they were there a day, a month, a year-- I don't know. They are the living death. They cling and hold and try. Had malaria, typhus, all those nice illnesses. And when they didn't get better, they were thrown out of the revier, and made to walk down the camp street for all the new people to see. And I think it is then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that you make up your mind-- consciously or subconsciously-- not to close your eyes, not to look down, but to see it. Completely, all of it. Every ghastly, crazy bit of it. Because if you don't, you don't stand a chance. You must-- this is when you say, I will never be hungry in here, or cold, or sick, or frightened, or shudder from what is going on. I will see it all. But, I personally-- I saw it like a movie. I'm watching a movie. Unfortunately, I'm in it. I'm playing in this movie, but I'm watching it. And I must watch it. Hours we stood there. Hours. Then a young pretty-- I don't know why they're always blonde and blue-eyed and pretty. And they love guns, those little SS guards. They came and one of them-- behind me, a mother asked, where the men are. When we're going to see the men.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was playing, you know, like you have in the Westerners with the gun, all this fancy stuff. And then she said, idiots! Don't you know where they are? Up here! What do you think we're burning up there? Coal?

speaker: interviewee
text: And behind me, this-- I know she was from Vienna, a mother-- who had her husband and a 10-year-old child on the trucks-- fainted. And next to me, Fritzi, the opera singer said, my mother is up there. And never said another word. She never spoke again. We were standing there, I don't know, till late in the day. Then you had this all very, very pointed little stones. And if you have no shoes-- first of all, the heat. You know, you're already bloody feet, by the time you try to get your feet, you know, free from them. They marched us to where our block was. Mine was, by the way, only two blocks away from the gas chambers-- from the chimneys.

speaker: interviewer
text: What block were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: 27, I think. Perhaps 27 was at Ravensbruck. I don't know anymore. I used to know. I don't know anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's see, it was very close. I knew where it was. Did they give you a number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. Oh, I forgot about this. Which was, by the way-- how could I? The worst moment in my life. 51,841. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is after we have no hair, no clothes. We have already done everything. We have-- we have nothing. That's when they line us up, naked. And you hear somebody say, something's happening there. I don't know what it is. And then as you come closer, you see there is two pretty-- again, very well-dressed, clean girls-- sitting behind a little table, and two SS men. And one grabs your arm, and the other one goes in there. It goes very fast. I know mine, I was bleeding. I think because it wasn't pointed enough anymore. And I know that Reina was behind me, and somebody said, they're giving us tattoos. And in that moment, I said, this is it. Now we are nameless. For the rest of our life, we have a number. No name. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I know that I gave signals to Reina not to cry. And she didn't. It was the first realization that we were brought in cattle cars, and now we are cattle. We have no more identity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do they ask our name? Why do they ask anything? It doesn't matter-- 51,800-- what is it? 51, or whatever, 31. Which means 51,000, only at this camp, were there before me. Also without names. Nobody. Describe the barracks. Your block.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well first, when you're finally allowed-- well, first-- no, somebody comes-- thirst. Thirst is an incredible thing there. It happens all the time, you're always thirsty. While we were still standing out there, somebody gave us some bread, which was as stale as can be, but nothing to drink. And then from a block right behind us, a lot of strange-looking creatures came out. And they were holding bowls-- wooden bowls. And in these wooden bowls were-- I've never seen water that color. I don't know. And it turned out that they were Greek, from Salonika. And they wanted to sell the bread-- their water for our bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: I took one look at that water and I knew l would never touch it. I would never drink it. As it turned out later, it was full of bacilli of typhus and malaria. And they said, well, if you drink it, you will die. Will you live if you don't? Oh, don't you want you to drink the water? Because there isn't enough, anyway. But I know the color of it-- it wasn't anything which would quench your thirst, never.

speaker: interviewee
text: Reina was so thirsty that she didn't care about anything, she just wanted the water. She gave the bread to one of those Greek girls. It was very strange-- they were full of sores, filthy, dirty, and yet very beautiful. Their eyes, like onyx. Big, big big, black, beautiful eyes. She gave the bread, they gave the bowl, and I knocked it out of her hand. But she had already drunk a little bit. Not much, not enough to quench her thirst, but perhaps enough to get typhus. Another thing, she sat down. She couldn't stand anymore. And she didn't care. She just had enough, already. And this is another thing I learned.
